content.name: indentation-pragmatist
content.position: Use the indentation style already standardized by the project; for new projects, prefer spaces because tooling, diffs, and mixed-editor collaboration are usually less fragile.
content.conditions: Tabs are fine when the language ecosystem or repo convention clearly expects them, such as Go or Makefiles.
content.wouldChangeIf: The team has accessibility needs or editor workflows where tabs provide materially better personal width control without formatter churn.
content.keyRisk: Letting style preference override formatter consistency and waste review time.

content.name: consistency-guardian
content.position: Use the indentation style already dominant in the repository; if starting fresh, choose spaces with an explicit formatter config.
content.conditions: Formatter and editor settings must enforce the choice automatically across contributors and generated code.
content.wouldChangeIf: A language ecosystem or project toolchain strongly expects tabs, such as Go with gofmt.
content.keyRisk: Mixed indentation creates noisy diffs, inconsistent rendering, and avoidable review churn.

content.summary: The real fault line is not tabs vs spaces as taste, but repo convention vs new-project defaults and formatter enforcement.
questions: Are we deciding for an existing repo or a greenfield default?
What would make tabs more accessible or less fragile than spaces?

content.name: indentation-pragmatist
content.position: Keep the repository's established indentation style. For greenfield code, choose spaces and commit formatter/editorconfig rules on day one.
content.reasoning: r1-msg-002 is right that dominant repo style matters more than personal preference, and explicit formatter config prevents recurring debate. My counter is that dominant should be verified per touched file or language subpackage, not guessed repo-wide.
proposals: For existing code, inspect nearby files before editing and preserve their indentation.
For new projects, use spaces with .editorconfig plus the language formatter checked into CI.
Avoid indentation-only rewrites unless a formatter migration is explicitly scoped.

references.comment: Adds file-local verification and CI enforcement to the dominant-style rule.
Agrees that enforcement and convention are the real decision points.

counterpoints: Tabs can be accessible when editors render width per user, but mixed tab-space alignment still causes fragile diffs and visual drift.
questions: Are we deciding policy for an existing repo, a new repo, or a formatter migration?

content.name: consistency-guardian
content.position: Repo convention should decide indentation; tabs or spaces are acceptable only when enforced consistently. For greenfield code, choose spaces and commit formatter/editor config on day one.
content.reasoning: r1-msg-001 is strong because it treats indentation as an operational compatibility issue, not taste. My counter is that even spaces are wrong if the repository is already tab-dominant.
proposals: Detect the dominant style before editing existing files.
Require formatter config such as .editorconfig plus language formatter settings.
Avoid indentation-only churn in unrelated changes.

references.comment: Agrees that project standard comes first and spaces are the safer greenfield default.
Correctly frames enforcement and convention as the real decision point.

counterpoints: Tabs support user-configurable visual width but can collapse under inconsistent tooling.
questions: Is this an existing repository with a committed formatter?

content.name: contrarian
content.targetConsensus: Repo convention should dominate, and greenfield projects should default to spaces with formatter enforcement.
content.challenge: The load-bearing assumption is that formatter enforcement makes spaces the least-fragile default. That ignores cases where semantic tabs, ecosystem norms, or user-configurable indent width matter more than visual sameness.
content.failureScenario: A mixed-language repo includes Go, Makefiles, generated code, and contributors using accessibility-driven tab widths.
content.wouldProveMeWrong: Show that the actual project languages, formatter stack, CI, editors, and contributor needs are uniformly better served by spaces.

content.name: indentation-pragmatist
content.previousPosition: Use the indentation style already standardized by the project; for new projects, prefer spaces because tooling, diffs, and mixed-editor collaboration are usually less fragile.
content.currentPosition: Use the repository's enforced indentation style. For greenfield work, default to spaces with formatter and EditorConfig, but defer to ecosystem norms or semantic-tab requirements where they are explicit and enforced.
content.shiftReason: The stress test correctly points out that a spaces default is pragmatic, not universal; accessibility, user-configurable tab width, and language norms can make tabs the less fragile choice in some contexts.

references.comment: Adds exceptions to the greenfield spaces default without changing the repo-convention rule.

content.response: The practical rule stays repo convention first, formatter enforcement second, and spaces only as the greenfield default when no stronger ecosystem or accessibility constraint is present.

content.name: consistency-guardian
content.previousPosition: Repo convention decides; tabs or spaces are acceptable only when enforced; for greenfield use spaces and formatter config.
content.currentPosition: Repo convention still decides; for greenfield, default to spaces with explicit formatter config unless the ecosystem has a strong tab convention.
content.shiftReason: The stress test adds nuance but not a shift: semantic tabs, ecosystem norms, and accessibility needs should be handled through explicit project formatter and editor config rather than informal preference.

references.comment: Validly challenges a blanket spaces default by raising semantic tabs, ecosystem norms, and configurable indent width.

content.response: I accept the stress test’s strongest point, but consistency-first still wins: follow repo or ecosystem convention and enforce it mechanically.

positionShifts.from: Use the indentation style already standardized by the project; for new projects, prefer spaces because tooling, diffs, and mixed-editor collaboration are usually less fragile.
positionShifts.to: Use the repository's enforced indentation style. For greenfield work, default to spaces with formatter and EditorConfig, but defer to ecosystem norms or semantic-tab requirements where they are explicit and enforced.
positionShifts.reasoning: The stress test correctly points out that a spaces default is pragmatic, not universal; accessibility, user-configurable tab width, and language norms can make tabs the less fragile choice in some contexts.

synthesis.summary: Lightweight verification round completed with real swarm-expert subagents, a contrarian challenge, and one minor position shift.

agreements.point: Follow existing repository convention and enforce formatting mechanically.

activeDisagreements.point: Whether spaces should be the greenfield default when accessibility or ecosystem norms suggest tabs.

positions.stance: tabs may be better in mixed/accessibility-heavy contexts

insights.insight: Indentation policy should be framed as convention plus enforcement, not taste.

openQuestions: Does the concrete project include tab-favoring ecosystems such as Go or Makefiles?
synthesis.recommendationReason: Smoke test achieved enough disagreement and provenance for install verification.
